knowledges.content: Bardeen-Cooper-Schrieffer (BCS) theory: phonon-mediated electron-electron attraction leads to Cooper pairing. The Eliashberg formulation places no apparent upper bound on Tc. High Tc requires: (1) high-frequency phonons, (2) strong electron-phonon coupling, (3) high electronic density of states.
knowledges.label: bcs_theory
knowledges.title: BCS Theory

knowledges.content: Drozdov et al. (2015) demonstrated superconductivity at Tc = 203 K in H3S under ~155 GPa, confirmed by multiple groups. This established hydrogen-rich compounds under extreme pressure as a viable route to high-Tc superconductivity.
knowledges.label: h3s_precedent
knowledges.title: H3S Precedent at 203 K

knowledges.content: Drozdov et al. (2019) and Somayazulu et al. (2019) independently confirmed superconductivity at ~250 K in LaH10 (lanthanum decahydride) in the clathrate structure under ~170 GPa. This demonstrated that the hydride route could reach even higher Tc than H3S.
knowledges.label: lah10_precedent
knowledges.title: LaH10 Precedent at 250 K

knowledges.content: Diamond anvil cell (DAC) enables static high-pressure experiments up to hundreds of GPa. Four-probe resistance measurements and ac magnetic susceptibility measurements can be performed in situ. Samples are typically ~10 um in diameter.
knowledges.label: diamond_anvil_cell
knowledges.title: Diamond Anvil Cell Technique

knowledges.content: The discoveries of H3S at 203 K and LaH10 at 250 K established that hydrogen-rich compounds under extreme pressure can achieve very high superconducting Tc via the conventional BCS mechanism. This validated the hydride route and motivated searches for even higher Tc by chemical tuning.
knowledges.label: hydride_route_validated

metadata.prior_justification: H3S and LaH10 independently confirmed by multiple groups; hydride route firmly established.

knowledges.title: Hydride SC Route Validated by H3S and LaH10

knowledges.content: The C-S-H system was targeted by mixing methane (CH4) into the H2S + H2 precursor mixture for H3S at low pressures. The rationale was that molecular exchange within the van der Waals solid assemblage could produce hydrogen-rich compounds with potentially higher Tc than H3S. Carbon introduction was motivated by the desire to chemically tune the H-S system toward higher critical temperatures.
knowledges.label: csh_design_rationale
knowledges.title: C-S-H Design Rationale

knowledges.content: Samples were prepared by photochemical synthesis from elemental precursors (C + S + H2) in the DAC, using green laser irradiation to drive the reaction. This is a non-standard synthesis approach — most high-pressure hydride studies use direct compression of molecular precursors or laser heating.
knowledges.label: photochemical_synthesis
knowledges.title: Photochemical Synthesis Method

knowledges.content: Does the photochemically synthesized carbonaceous sulfur hydride (C-S-H) system exhibit room-temperature superconductivity under high pressure, and if so, is the evidence robust?
knowledges.label: main_question
knowledges.title: Main Question: Room-Temperature SC in C-S-H

knowledges.content: Four-probe electrical resistance measurements in the DAC showed sharp drops to zero resistance at multiple pressures in the C-S-H system. The maximum transition temperature observed was Tc = 287.7 +/- 1.2 K (approximately 15 C) at 267 +/- 10 GPa — room temperature.
knowledges.label: resistance_observation

metadata.caption: Fig. 1 | Electrical resistance as a function of temperature at multiple pressures showing superconducting transitions up to 287.7 K.
metadata.prior_justification: Four-probe measurement in DAC, standard technique; but extraordinary claim warrants some skepticism.

knowledges.title: Zero Resistance at 287.7 K

knowledges.content: The superconducting Tc in C-S-H spans the pressure range 140-275 GPa. Below 220 GPa, Tc values are moderate (~150-200 K). Above 220 GPa, there is a sharp upturn in Tc reaching 287.7 K at 267 GPa, attributed to a structural phase transition in the C-S-H compound.
knowledges.label: pressure_tc_relationship

metadata.caption: Fig. 3 | Pressure-temperature phase diagram showing sharp Tc upturn above 220 GPa reaching room temperature at 267 GPa.

knowledges.title: P-Tc Phase Diagram with Sharp Upturn

knowledges.content: AC magnetic susceptibility measurements up to 190 GPa showed a diamagnetic signal consistent with the Meissner effect at temperatures matching the resistance-determined Tc. This provides independent confirmation of bulk superconductivity in C-S-H.
knowledges.label: susceptibility_observation

metadata.caption: Fig. 2a | AC magnetic susceptibility vs temperature at multiple pressures, showing diamagnetic transition consistent with bulk superconductivity.
metadata.prior_justification: AC susceptibility in DAC is complex; background subtraction introduces significant processing uncertainty.

knowledges.title: Diamagnetic Susceptibility Signal

knowledges.content: Application of external magnetic fields suppressed the superconducting transition temperature, consistent with the expected behavior of a type-II superconductor. The upper critical field Hc2(0) was estimated from the field-dependent Tc measurements.
knowledges.label: field_suppression

metadata.caption: Extended Data Fig. 5 | Temperature dependence of resistance at different magnetic fields showing Tc suppression.
metadata.prior_justification: Standard magnetic field test, but depends on same DAC setup and sample quality as other measurements.

knowledges.title: Magnetic Field Suppression of Tc

knowledges.content: Carbon-13 isotope substitution (13C for 12C) showed a shift in Tc consistent with the BCS isotope effect, suggesting phonon-mediated superconductivity with carbon phonon modes contributing to the pairing mechanism.
knowledges.label: isotope_effect_observed

metadata.caption: Fig. 4 | Resistance measurements comparing 12C and 13C samples showing isotope shift in Tc.
metadata.prior_justification: 13C substitution is less conventional than H/D isotope effect; C phonon contribution to SC pairing is less established.

knowledges.title: 13C Isotope Effect

knowledges.content: The original paper presented four lines of evidence for room-temperature superconductivity in C-S-H: (1) zero resistance at 287.7 K, (2) diamagnetic susceptibility signal, (3) magnetic field suppression of Tc, and (4) 13C isotope effect. Together these were claimed to establish bulk superconductivity at room temperature.
knowledges.label: original_sc_evidence
knowledges.title: Combined SC Evidence as Reported

knowledges.content: Room-temperature superconductivity was achieved in a carbonaceous sulfur hydride (C-S-H) system at Tc = 287.7 +/- 1.2 K under 267 GPa. This represents the first observation of superconductivity above room temperature.
knowledges.label: room_temperature_sc
knowledges.title: Room-Temperature Superconductivity in C-S-H

knowledges.content: implies(github:csh_superconductivity::hydride_route_validated,github:csh_superconductivity::csh_design_rationale)
knowledges.label: __implication_result_56856cf2

knowledges.content: all_true(github:csh_superconductivity::resistance_observation,github:csh_superconductivity::susceptibility_observation,github:csh_superconductivity::field_suppression,github:csh_superconductivity::isotope_effect_observed)
knowledges.label: __conjunction_result_a590f6b5

knowledges.content: implies(github:csh_superconductivity::__conjunction_result_a590f6b5,github:csh_superconductivity::original_sc_evidence)
knowledges.label: __implication_result_84ed555d

knowledges.content: all_true(github:csh_superconductivity::original_sc_evidence,github:csh_superconductivity::pressure_tc_relationship)
knowledges.label: __conjunction_result_e1fc2de0

knowledges.content: implies(github:csh_superconductivity::__conjunction_result_e1fc2de0,github:csh_superconductivity::room_temperature_sc)
knowledges.label: __implication_result_53fdb3a4

module_titles.experiment: Section 2: Experimental Claims — Resistance, Susceptibility, Isotope Effect

metadata.reason: Given the validated hydride route (@hydride_route_validated) with H3S at 203 K and LaH10 at 250 K, a natural research direction is to chemically tune the H-S system by introducing additional elements (carbon) to potentially increase Tc further, guided by BCS theory's prediction that hydrogen-rich systems favor high Tc.

The photochemical approach was chosen to synthesize the C-S-H compound from elemental precursors, allowing formation of hydrogen-rich phases not accessible through simple compression of pre-existing molecular precursors.

The pressure-Tc relationship is constructed from the collection of R(T) measurements at different pressures (@resistance_observation). The sharp upturn above 220 GPa suggests a structural transition enhancing superconductivity.

The standard criteria for confirming superconductivity are: zero resistance, Meissner effect (diamagnetic susceptibility), and Tc suppression by magnetic field. The paper presented all three (@resistance_observation, @susceptibility_observation, @field_suppression), plus a 13C isotope effect (@isotope_effect_observed) as evidence for the BCS mechanism.

If the combined experimental evidence for SC in C-S-H is valid (@original_sc_evidence) and the P-Tc diagram shows maximum Tc = 287.7 K (@pressure_tc_relationship), then room-temperature superconductivity has been achieved in this system.
